name: Fiber
what_it_does: Express.js-inspired Go web framework built on fasthttp (not net/http). Familiar API for Node.js developers transitioning to Go. Claims the fastest Go framework by raw HTTP throughput due to fasthttp's zero-allocation design. Includes middleware, file serving, WebSocket, JWT, and rate limiting via middleware packages.
use_cases: Build Go agent HTTP APIs with Express-like routing syntax for teams familiar with Node.js patterns
Create high-throughput Go agent microservices where raw HTTP performance (fasthttp backend) matters most
Port Express.js agent backends to Go with minimal routing API changes
Implement Go agent REST APIs with Fiber's rich built-in middleware (JWT, CORS, rate limiting, cache)
Build WebSocket-based agent communication endpoints in Go with Fiber's WebSocket middleware
not_for: Applications using standard net/http middleware — Fiber's fasthttp base is incompatible with net/http middleware; Gin or Chi are better for net/http compatibility
Production services requiring net/http compatibility — many Go libraries and proxies assume net/http; Fiber requires adaptation
Teams preferring idiomatic Go patterns — Fiber's Express-style API is non-idiomatic Go; Gin or Chi are more Go-idiomatic
best_when: You need maximum raw HTTP throughput in Go and your team has Express.js background — Fiber's familiar API reduces the Go learning curve.
avoid_when: You need net/http middleware compatibility (common Go libraries assume net/http) — use Gin or Chi for better ecosystem compatibility.

auth.notes: Web framework — auth via Fiber JWT middleware or custom middleware.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits built-in (middleware available). fasthttp backend delivers highest throughput of any Go framework in many benchmarks.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Handler errors use Fiber's error types. c.JSON() and c.Status() control response. Custom error handler configurable for consistent API error shapes.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: HTTP framework — idempotency is application responsibility.
known_agent_gotchas: fasthttp is NOT compatible with net/http — Fiber cannot use net/http handlers or middleware directly; this is the biggest ecosystem limitation vs. Gin/Chi
ctx.Body() and ctx.Params() strings are only valid during the handler — strings become invalid after the handler returns due to fasthttp's buffer reuse; always copy strings you need to retain
Fiber uses goroutines with fasthttp's connection pool — Go race conditions in handler code are possible; use mutex or channels for shared state
Fiber 2.x to 3.x (in development) has significant API changes — pin versions carefully; 3.x betas break 2.x code
WebSocket in Fiber uses a different handler signature than net/http-based WebSocket libraries — code must be adapted for Fiber's c.Locals() pattern for passing data
Testing Fiber applications with httptest is not directly possible (no net/http compatibility) — use Fiber's built-in test utilities (app.Test()) instead

security_score_components.security_notes: Go memory safety. fasthttp's buffer reuse requires careful string handling to prevent data leaks between requests. TLS via standard Go net/tls.
